facts_disclosed: Switched to Thai Star feed, 295,000 dong/25kg bag

turns.content: friend i want to ask you something. i switch feed brand for my shrimp, you know. want to know if i do right thing
Sure, I'd be happy to help you think it through. What did you switch to, and what made you decide to change?
i use Thai Star now. 295,000 dong for 25 kilo bag. my neighbor Hung he use it already 6 month and his shrimp look good, you know. grow normal, no problem
That's a good sign that your neighbor has had 6 months of success with it — that's a real-world trial right next door. What were you paying before with your old feed?

before i pay much more, you know. price keep going up every year. Thai Star same 25 kilo bag but big difference in price. i save maybe 500,000 dong per week across all 3 pond. that is real money friend

500,000 dong per week is significant — that's over 2 million per month and roughly 26 million dong per year in savings. That's nothing to ignore.

The key question with any feed switch is whether the growth rate and feed conversion ratio (FCR) stay the same. A cheaper feed that takes longer to grow the shrimp to market size can actually cost more in the end. 

How long have you been on Thai Star now? Are you seeing the same growth as before?
about 3 week now. so far shrimp eating good, you know. they take all the feed from tray, no leftover. and color look normal. but 3 week still early, i know

You're right that 3 weeks is still early, but the signs are positive. The fact that they're eating all of it and leaving nothing on the tray is a great indicator — shrimp are actually pretty picky, and if the feed quality is poor, they'll leave it.

Here's what I'd suggest monitoring over the next month or two:

1. **Weekly sampling** — weigh 20-30 random shrimp each week to track average growth rate
2. **FCR** — keep track of total feed used vs. estimated biomass gain
3. **Shell quality** — look for soft shells or discoloration, which can signal missing nutrients
4. **Mortality** — any unusual die-off could point to nutritional deficiency

Are you keeping records of the weight samples?
i write in my notebook every week. weight, how much feed, how many die. 40 year i do this, you know. my notebook is my computer ha

Ha, the notebook works! 40 years of data in those pages — that's worth more than any software. As long as you're tracking it consistently, you'll be able to compare this cycle to previous ones.

After about 6-8 weeks on Thai Star, look at your numbers and compare to the same growth stage with your old feed. If the average weight gain per week is similar and survival rate is the same, then you've found yourself a real savings with no downside.

Did Hung mention anything about the FCR he's getting with Thai Star?
Hung say he get about 1.4 FCR. that is okay, not amazing but not bad. he also say shrimp shell look thick and strong. i trust Hung, you know. he farm next to me 20 year. he not lie about these thing

1.4 FCR is solid for vannamei — anything under 1.5 is considered good in the industry. And thick shells are a positive sign that the calcium and mineral content in the feed is adequate.

You're in a good position: a trusted neighbor with 6 months of real results, your own shrimp are accepting the feed well after 3 weeks, and you're saving 500,000 dong per week. That's a strong case.

The fact that you trust Hung and can actually see his shrimp is worth more than any marketing from a feed company. Real results from the pond next door — you can't fake that.
exactly friend. i not believe salesman. salesman always say best feed, number one quality, you know. but i believe what i see with my eye. Hung shrimp look good, size look good. that is enough for me

That's the smartest approach. You've got 40 years of experience reading shrimp — your eyes and your notebook are better than any sales pitch.

Just keep doing what you're doing: track the numbers, compare after a couple months, and if Thai Star holds up, you've just saved yourself 26 million dong a year. That's a meaningful difference for the operation.
yes 26 million dong per year, that is one worker salary almost, you know. very important. okay friend thank you. i keep watching the number and see. if problem i come back ask you

Anytime! You're doing everything right — switching based on evidence, tracking the data, and staying watchful. Come back if you notice anything off or just want to compare your numbers. Good luck with the Thai Star.
